title: Isaiah 52
subtitle: Beautiful Feet and the Suffering Servant Begins

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — “How Beautiful on the Mountains Are the Feet”

heb.gloss: one who brings good news / herald
heb.paragraph: "How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news (məbasher), who proclaim peace, who bring good tidings, who proclaim salvation, who say to Zion, ‘Your God reigns!’" (v.7). Paul quotes this in Romans 10:15 for the preaching of the gospel. "Awake, awake, Zion, put on your strength! Put on your garments of splendour, Jerusalem!" (v.1). "Burst into songs of joy together, you ruins of Jerusalem, for the LORD has comforted his people, he has redeemed Jerusalem" (v.9). "The LORD will lay bare his holy arm in the sight of all the nations, and all the ends of the earth will see the salvation of our God" (v.10). "Depart, depart, go out from there! Touch no unclean thing!" (v.11). The new exodus: leave Babylon, but not in haste like the first exodus — "for the LORD will go before you, the God of Israel will be your rear guard" (v.12).

notes.note: "Awake, awake, Zion! Free yourself from the chains on your neck." The city told to dress up and break free. Captivity is ending.

"How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news, who proclaim peace, who say, Your God reigns!" Romans 10:15: the gospel-preaching verse. The feet are beautiful because of the message they carry. The message has four parts: good news, peace, good tidings, salvation.
"The LORD will lay bare his holy arm in the sight of all the nations." The arm of 51:9 is bared. The nations will see. Salvation is public, not private.

"You will not leave in haste or go in flight; for the LORD will go before you, and the God of Israel will be your rear guard." The new exodus surpasses the first: no haste, no flight. God is both vanguard and rearguard.

refs.note: Paul quotes 'how beautiful are the feet' to describe gospel messengers — the good-news herald tradition.
Nahum uses the same beautiful-feet formula for the messenger announcing Nineveh's fall — shared victory-announcement genre.
Zion as herald of good news — the bookend oracle that Isaiah 52 now fulfills.
Come out from them and be separate, touch no unclean thing — Paul's adaptation of the exodus-purity call.

hist.context: Chapter 52 opens with another 'awake, awake' summons, this time to Zion herself. Put on your beautiful garments, holy city — the uncircumcised and unclean will no longer enter (v.1). The beautiful-feet oracle (vv.7–10) celebrates the messenger running over the mountains with good news: your God reigns! The watchmen on Zion's walls see YHWH returning and break into song. The exodus call follows: depart, touch nothing unclean, go out in purity but not in haste (v.11–12). YHWH himself goes before and behind — rearguard and vanguard for the returning exiles.

sections.header: Verses 13–15 — “My Servant Will Be Raised and Lifted Up: The Fourth Servant Song Begins”

heb.paragraph: "See, my servant will act wisely; he will be raised and lifted up and highly exalted. Just as there were many who were appalled at him — his appearance was so disfigured (mashchit) beyond that of any human being and his form marred beyond human likeness — so he will sprinkle many nations, and kings will shut their mouths because of him" (vv.13–15). THE FOURTH SERVANT SONG BEGINS (52:13–53:12). Three verses that frame the entire song. The servant’s trajectory: exalted (v.13) → disfigured (v.14) → exalting nations (v.15). The disfigurement is beyond human — the suffering exceeds normal experience. Kings are speechless. The cross before the cross.

notes.note: "My servant will act wisely; he will be raised and lifted up and highly exalted." Three verbs of elevation: raised, lifted, exalted. The same verbs used of God in 6:1 ("high and exalted"). The servant shares God’s position.
"His appearance was so disfigured beyond that of any human being." The suffering that makes the servant unrecognisable. The Passion narrative: scourging, thorns, nails produced literal disfigurement.

"He will sprinkle many nations, and kings will shut their mouths." Sprinkling: the priestly purification act (Lev 14:7; Num 19:18). The servant is both sacrifice and priest. Kings are silenced — not by power but by the spectacle of suffering that redeems.

refs.note: The exaltation language — raised, lifted up, highly exalted — shapes Paul's Christ hymn; the servant pattern reversed in glory.
John quotes 'who has believed our message' to explain Israel's unbelief at Jesus's signs — the servant's rejection anticipated.
The Ethiopian eunuch reads Isaiah 53; Philip begins with this scripture and tells him the good news about Jesus.
Peter applies the wound-healing and sin-bearing language directly to Christ's atoning work on the cross.

hist.context: The Fourth Servant Song begins at 52:13 (many scholars treat 52:13–53:12 as a unit). The Servant will be raised and highly exalted (v.13), yet his appearance was marred beyond human likeness (v.14). Kings will shut their mouths at him; what they were never told they will see (v.15). This prologue sets up the shocking reversal: the despised figure is the exalted agent of YHWH's salvation. The nations and kings who were horrified will be silenced with awe. The passage is the theological climax of Deutero-Isaiah.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHow beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news, who proclaim peace, who bring good tidings, who proclaim salvation, who say to Zion, “Your God reigns!”ESVHow beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace, who brings good news of happiness, who publishes salvation, who says to Zion, “Your God reigns.”NIVFor this is what the Sovereign Lord says: “At first my people went down to Egypt to live; lately, Assyria has oppressed them.ESVFor thus says the Lord GOD: “My people went down at the first into Egypt to sojourn there, and the Assyrian oppressed them for nothing.

rec.text: ""Awake, awake, Zion! Free yourself from the chains on your neck." The city told to dress up and break free. Captivity is ending."

rows.label: The Fourth Song Begins — 52:13–15
rows.text: "RAISED and lifted up." "Disfigured BEYOND HUMAN LIKENESS." "Kings will shut their mouths." The cross before the cross.

hebtext.gloss: one who brings good news / herald
hebtext.note: "How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news (məbasher), who proclaim peace, who bring good tidings, who proclaim salvation, who say to Zion, ‘Your God reigns!’" (v.7). Just as there were many who were appalled at him — his appearance was so disfigured (mashchit) beyond that of any human being and his form marred beyond human likeness — so he will sprinkle many nations, and kings will shut their mouths because of him" (vv.13–15). The disfigurement is beyond human — the suffering exceeds normal experience. Kings are speechless. The cross before the cross.

themes.note: Prophecy AND Mission peak at 10: beautiful feet (Rom 10:15), the fourth Servant Song begins. "Your God reigns!" Nations sprinkled. Kings silenced. The theological climax of the OT approaches.

coaching.tip: 'How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news' (v. 7). Paul quotes this in Romans 10:15 to describe gospel preaching. The original context is a messenger running from Babylon to Jerusalem with news that the exile is over. The feet are beautiful because of where they've been and what they carry. The gospel is always someone bringing news from far away that changes everything nearby.

The fourth Servant Song begins at 52:13 — 'my servant will be raised and lifted up and highly exalted.' The same verbs (raised, lifted, exalted) describe God himself in Isaiah 6:1. Isaiah uses divine language for the Servant. By the time chapter 53 ends, the reader must ask: who is this figure who suffers as a human but is described as God? The question is the point.
